title: In Utrumque Paratus - A Logical Discussion
content:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Then hey for boot and horse, lad!
آ آ آ آ And round the world away!
آ آ آ آ Young blood will have its course, lad!
آ آ آ آ And every dog his day!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ - C. Kingsley.


آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s a formula which the west country clowns
آ آ آ آ Once used, ere their blows fell thick,
آ آ آ آ At the fairs on the Devon and Cornwall downs,
آ آ آ آ In their bouts with the single-stick.
آ آ آ آ You may read a moral, not far amiss,
آ آ آ آ If you care to moralise,
آ آ آ آ In the crossing-guard, where the ash-plants kiss,
آ آ آ آ To the words ï؟½God spare our eyesï؟½.
آ آ آ آ No game was ever yet worth a rap
آ آ آ آ For a rational man to play,
آ آ آ آ Into which no accident, no mishap,
آ آ آ آ Could possibly find its way.

آ آ آ آ If you hold the willow, a shooter from Wills
آ آ آ آ May transform you into a hopper,
آ آ آ آ And the football meadow is rife with spills,
آ آ آ آ If you feel disposed for a cropper;
آ آ آ آ In a rattling gallop with hound and horse
آ آ آ آ You may chance to reverse the medal
آ آ آ آ On the sward, with the saddle your loins across,
آ آ آ آ And your hunterï؟½s loins on the saddle;
آ آ آ آ In the stubbles youï؟½ll find it hard to frame
آ آ آ آ A remonstrance firm, yet civil,
آ آ آ آ When oft as ï؟½our mutual friendï؟½ takes aim,
آ آ آ آ Long odds may be laid on the rising game,
آ آ آ آ And against your gaiters level;
آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s danger even where fish are caught,
آ آ آ آ To those who a wetting fear;
آ آ آ آ For whatï؟½s worth having must aye be bought,
آ آ آ آ And sportï؟½s like life and lifeï؟½s like sport,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½It ainï؟½t all skittles and beer.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ The honey bag lies close to the sting,
آ آ آ آ The rose is fenced by the thorn,
آ آ آ آ Shall we leave to others their gathering,
آ آ آ آ And turn from clustering fruits that cling
آ آ آ آ To the garden wall in scorn?
آ آ آ آ Albeit those purple grapes hang high,
آ آ آ آ Like the fox in the ancient tale,
آ آ آ آ Let us pause and try, ere we pass them by,
آ آ آ آ Though we, like the fox, may fail.

آ آ آ آ All hurry is worse than useless; think
آ آ آ آ On the adage, ï؟½ï؟½Tis pace that killsï؟½;
آ آ آ آ Shun bad tobacco, avoid strong drink,
آ آ آ آ Abstain from Hollowayï؟½s pills,
آ آ آ آ Wear woollen socks, theyï؟½re the best youï؟½ll find,
آ آ آ آ Beware how you leave off flannel;
آ آ آ آ And whatever you do, donï؟½t change your mind
آ آ آ آ When once you have picked your panel;
آ آ آ آ With a bank of cloud in the south south-east,
آ آ آ آ Stand ready to shorten sail;
آ آ آ آ Fight shy of a corporation feast;
آ آ آ آ Donï؟½t trust to a martingale;
آ آ آ آ Keep your powder dry, and shut one eye,
آ آ آ آ Not both, when you touch your trigger;
آ آ آ آ Donï؟½t stop with your head too frequently
آ آ آ آ (This advice ainï؟½t meant for a nigger);
آ آ آ آ Look before you leap, if you like, but if
آ آ آ آ You mean leaping, donï؟½t look long,
آ آ آ آ Or the weakest place will soon grow stiff,
آ آ آ آ And the strongest doubly strong;
آ آ آ آ As far as you can, to every man,
آ آ آ آ Let your aid be freely given,
آ آ آ آ And hit out straight, ï؟½tis your shortest plan,
آ آ آ آ When against the ropes youï؟½re driven.

آ آ آ آ Mere pluck, though not in the least sublime,
آ آ آ آ Is wiser than blank dismay,
آ آ آ آ Since ï؟½No sparrow can fall before its timeï؟½,
آ آ آ آ And weï؟½re valued higher than they;
آ آ آ آ So hope for the best and leave the rest
آ آ آ آ In charge of a stronger hand,
آ آ آ آ Like the honest boors in the far-off west,
آ آ آ آ With the formula terse and grand.

آ آ آ آ They were men for the most part rough and rude,
آ آ آ آ Dull and illiterate,
آ آ آ آ But they nursed no quarrel, they cherished no feud,
آ آ آ آ They were strangers to spite and hate;
آ آ آ آ In a kindly spirit they took their stand,
آ آ آ آ That brothers and sons might learn
آ آ آ آ How a man should uphold the sports of his land,
آ آ آ آ And strike his best with a strong right hand,
آ آ آ آ And take his strokes in return.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½ï؟½Twas a barbarous practice,ï؟½ the Quaker cries,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½ï؟½Tis a thing of the past, thank heavenï؟½,
آ آ آ آ Keep your thanks till the combative instinct dies
آ آ آ آ With the taint of the olden leaven;
آ آ آ آ Yes, the times are changed, for better or worse,
آ آ آ آ The prayer that no harm befall
آ آ آ آ Has given its place to a drunken curse,
آ آ آ آ And the manly game to a brawl.

آ آ آ آ Our burdens are heavy, our natures weak,
آ آ آ آ Some pastime devoid of harm
آ آ آ آ May we look for? ï؟½Puritan elder, speak!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Yea, friend, peradventure thou mayest seek
آ آ آ آ Recreation singing a psalm.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ If I did, your visage so grim and stern
آ آ آ آ Would relax in a ghastly smile,
آ آ آ آ For of music I never one note could learn,
آ آ آ آ And my feeble minstrelsy would turn
آ آ آ آ Your chant to discord vile.

آ آ آ آ Thoï؟½ the Philistineï؟½s mail could not avail,
آ آ آ آ Nor the spear like a weaverï؟½s beam,
آ آ آ آ There are episodes yet in the Psalmistï؟½s tale,
آ آ آ آ To obliterate which his poems fail,
آ آ آ آ Which his exploits fail to redeem.
آ آ آ آ Can the Hittiteï؟½s wrongs forgotten be?
آ آ آ آ Does he warble ï؟½Non nobis Domineï؟½,
آ آ آ آ With his monarch in blissful concert, free
آ آ آ آ From all malice to flesh inherent;
آ آ آ آ Zeruiahï؟½s offspring, who served so well,
آ آ آ آ Yet between the horns of the altar fell,
آ آ آ آ Does his voice the ï؟½Quid gloriarisï؟½ swell,
آ آ آ آ Or the ï؟½Quare fremueruntï؟½?
آ آ آ آ It may well be thus where David sings,
آ آ آ آ And Uriah joins in the chorus,
آ آ آ آ But while earth to earthy matter clings,
آ آ آ آ Neither you nor the bravest of Judahï؟½s kings
آ آ آ آ As a pattern can stand before us.
meaning: About: Adam Lindsay Gordon was an Australian poet, jockey and politician.
